exercises.title: The three failure modes of square-resize
exercises.prompt: Pre-2024 VLMs resized every image to a fixed square (e.g. 224×224). Name the three failure modes of square-resize and why they break OCR, document understanding, and high-resolution scene parsing.
exercises.hint: Squished text (aspect-ratio distortion), cropped content (lost edges), wasted tokens on padding. Real images are 9:16 receipts, 16:9 charts, 4096px scans — none are squares.
exercises.solution:
Real images are **not 224×224 squares**: a receipt is 9:16, a chart is 16:9, a medical scan might be 4096×4096, a mobile screenshot is 9:19.5. Forcing them into a fixed square has **three failure modes:**

1. **Squished text (aspect-ratio distortion):** resizing a tall receipt or wide chart to a square **stretches/compresses** it, **distorting characters and shapes**. OCR fails because the text is no longer the shape the model expects — letters are squished, lines warped. Document understanding depends on faithful glyph shapes; distortion destroys them.

2. **Cropped content (lost information):** if instead of squishing you **center-crop** to a square, you **throw away the edges** — the top/bottom of a tall document, the sides of a wide one. Critical content (headers, margins, the right column of a chart) is **simply gone**, and the model can't reason about what it never saw.

3. **Wasted tokens on padding:** if you **pad** to a square instead, you spend **visual tokens on blank padding** — for a 9:19.5 screenshot padded to square, more than half the tokens encode **nothing**, wasting the (quadratic-cost) token budget on padding while *also* limiting the resolution you can afford for the actual content.

**Why this breaks the key tasks:** **OCR** needs **undistorted, high-resolution text** (squishing/cropping kills it); **document understanding** needs the **full page at faithful aspect ratio** (cropping/distortion loses layout and content); **high-resolution scene parsing** (small objects, fine detail) needs **enough resolution** without wasting it on padding or losing it to downscaling. Square-resize sacrifices exactly the signal these tasks depend on. The any-resolution methods (AnyRes, NaFlex, M-RoPE) exist to **preserve native aspect ratio and resolution** so this signal survives — which is why high-res OCR/document/chart performance jumped once VLMs stopped square-resizing.

exercises.title: Patch-n'-pack and block-diagonal masking
exercises.prompt: Implement patch-n'-pack (NaViT): pack patches from a batch of variable-resolution images into one sequence with a block-diagonal attention mask so images don't attend across each other. Explain why this enables variable-resolution training in a single batch and the efficiency win over padding.
exercises.hint: Concatenate all images' patches into one long sequence; build a mask where patches only attend within their own image (block-diagonal). No padding to a common size → no wasted compute. Enables mixed resolutions/aspect ratios in one batch.
exercises.solution:
```python
import numpy as np

def patch_n_pack(images_patches):
    """images_patches: list of arrays, each (n_i, d) for image i (variable n_i).
       Returns packed sequence + block-diagonal attention mask."""
    packed = np.concatenate(images_patches, axis=0)     # one long sequence
    sizes = [p.shape[0] for p in images_patches]
    total = sum(sizes)
    mask = np.zeros((total, total), dtype=bool)
    start = 0
    for n in sizes:                                       # block-diagonal
        mask[start:start+n, start:start+n] = True         # attend only within image
        start += n
    return packed, mask          # attention uses mask: -inf where mask is False
```

**Patch-n'-pack:** instead of padding every image to a common size and batching them as a tensor, **concatenate the patches of multiple variable-resolution images into a single long sequence**, and use a **block-diagonal attention mask** so each image's patches **attend only within their own image** (never across image boundaries) — like document-boundary masking in packed text (Phase 10.3). One forward pass processes many images of **different resolutions and aspect ratios** simultaneously.

**Why it enables variable-resolution training in one batch:** normally a batch is a **rectangular tensor**, so all images must share dimensions → you're forced to resize/pad to a common size (the failure modes of Exercise 1). Packing breaks that constraint: since everything is **one flat sequence with a mask**, images can have **any number of patches each** — a 196-patch thumbnail and a 2000-patch scan coexist in the same batch. The model trains on **native resolutions and aspect ratios**, learning to handle the real diversity of inputs rather than a distorted square approximation.

**The efficiency win over padding:** padding-to-square **wastes compute on padding tokens** — if you batch a 9:19.5 screenshot with a square photo, the screenshot is mostly padding, and the GPU computes attention/FFN over those meaningless tokens. Packing has **no padding**: every token in the sequence is **real content**, so **no compute is wasted**. NaViT showed this lets you train on **far more images per unit compute** (you're not paying for padding), and you can even **vary the token budget per image** (more tokens to important images, fewer to simple ones) within a packed batch. The cost is the **block-diagonal mask** (and position handling), but the result is **native-resolution training at higher throughput** — the foundation for the any-aspect-ratio encoders (SigLIP 2 NaFlex) that became the default.
exercises.starter_code:

def patch_n_pack(images_patches):
    # Concatenate variable-length patch sets into one sequence; build a
    # block-diagonal boolean mask (attend only within each image). Return
    # (packed, mask).
    pass

tests.desc: the mask is square over the packed sequence
tokens attend within their own image

exercises.title: AnyRes vs NaFlex vs M-RoPE: picking the right tool
exercises.prompt: Three approaches handle resolution differently: AnyRes tiling (LLaVA-NeXT), NaFlex (SigLIP 2), and M-RoPE (Qwen2-VL). Explain each, their tradeoffs, how to pick for a given task (OCR, charts, photography, video), and how token budgets factor in. Why did dropping absolute positional tables (M-RoPE) matter?
exercises.hint: AnyRes: tile a high-res image into a base + sub-images, encode each. NaFlex: native variable-resolution encoder (patch-n'-pack inside). M-RoPE: 2D/3D rotary positions, no absolute table → any resolution, extrapolates, video time axis. Pick by resolution need + token budget. Absolute tables can't handle unseen resolutions.
exercises.solution:
**The three approaches:**
- **AnyRes tiling (LLaVA-NeXT):** keep a **fixed-resolution encoder**, but **tile a high-resolution image into a grid of sub-images** (plus a downscaled 'base' overview), encode **each tile separately** at the encoder's native size, and concatenate all the tokens. *Tradeoff:* works with **any existing fixed-res encoder** (no retraining), gives **high effective resolution** (more tiles = more detail), but **token count grows with the number of tiles** (a 4×4 grid = 16× the tokens of one image, plus the base) — can explode the budget. Tiles also lose **cross-tile context** (each tile encoded independently). Simple and effective; the dominant practical hack.
- **NaFlex (SigLIP 2):** a **natively variable-resolution encoder** — patch-n'-pack (Exercise 2) baked into the encoder so **one checkpoint handles any aspect ratio/resolution** directly, no tiling. *Tradeoff:* **clean, no tiling artifacts, no cross-tile context loss**, single encoder for everything — but requires an **encoder trained this way** (you don't bolt it onto an old fixed-res model). The 2025 default for open VLMs wanting one checkpoint to serve every aspect ratio.
- **M-RoPE (Qwen2-VL):** **multi-axis rotary position encoding** — instead of a learned **absolute positional table**, encode position with **rotary embeddings along multiple axes** (height, width, and for video, **time**). *Tradeoff:* handles **any resolution** (positions are computed, not looked up), **extrapolates** to resolutions/lengths unseen in training, and **natively extends to video** (the temporal axis) — the positional half of native-resolution support, pairs with packing.

**Picking for a task:**
- **OCR / documents:** need **maximum faithful resolution** → **AnyRes tiling** (lots of tiles for dense text) or a **high-res NaFlex** encoder; never square-resize. Watch the token budget — dense documents are token-hungry.
- **Charts / diagrams:** need **resolution + correct aspect ratio** → NaFlex or AnyRes; M-RoPE for clean positions.
- **Photography / natural scenes:** lower resolution often suffices → **a single native-res pass (NaFlex)** is cheaper than heavy tiling; don't waste tokens.
- **Video:** need the **temporal axis** → **M-RoPE** (its time dimension), with frame sampling controlling tokens (Lessons 9, 17).

**How token budgets factor in:** every approach trades **detail (tokens) vs cost**. AnyRes's tiles and high native resolutions **multiply tokens** (quadratic attention, context limits, Lesson 1). So the real decision is **'how much resolution does this task need, and can I afford the tokens?'** — give OCR/documents a big budget (many tiles/high res), give simple photos a small one. This is exactly the **visual-token-budget** problem that LLaVA-OneVision (Lesson 8) formalizes (constant budget across input types) and that token-compression methods (Lessons 17-18) attack for video.

**Why dropping absolute positional tables (M-RoPE) mattered:** a **learned absolute positional table** has a **fixed maximum size** — it has entries only for positions seen in training, so it **can't represent resolutions/sequence lengths beyond that** (the same limit as learned text positions, Phase 7.4). For native-resolution vision, where images come in **arbitrary sizes**, an absolute table is a hard ceiling and requires interpolation hacks. **M-RoPE computes positions via rotation** (parameter-free, defined for any coordinate), so it **handles any resolution, extrapolates to unseen sizes, and cleanly adds a time axis for video** — exactly what native, any-resolution, any-length multimodal input demands. Dropping the absolute table was the positional enabler for 'one model, any aspect ratio, any length' — which is why Qwen2-VL's M-RoPE was copied across the open ecosystem within a year. **The synthesis:** native-resolution support = **flexible patching/packing (NaFlex/AnyRes) + flexible positions (M-RoPE) + a managed token budget** — together they let VLMs see real images at real resolutions without the square-resize failure modes.
